1:
‘And again he entered into Capernaum after some days; and it was noised that he was in the house.’ The witnesses agree on the eager resort to Christ and the setting of the paralytic’s healing, with the fathers drawing out its deeper sense.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the scene. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson identify the house as Peter’s, now Christ’s home in Capernaum (Mark 1:29), and note Mark’s vivid detail — the report spreading, ‘He is at home.’ Augustine harmonises Matthew’s ‘his own city’ (Matthew 9:1) with Mark’s Capernaum, the metropolis of Galilee.

The fathers probe the miracle to come. Bede marks the order — Christ ‘first loosed the chains of his sins’ before the joints, and in his ‘wonderful humility’ calls the despised man ‘son’ — and notes ‘how much each person’s own faith weighs with God,’ one man healed by another’s merit. Theophylact and Chrysostom hold Christ proved ‘the cure of the soul by that of the body,’ showing the invisible by the visible. Pseudo-Jerome reads the palsy as ‘the torpor in which man lies slothful in the softness of the flesh.’

The Puritan Henry observes the crowd that gathered ‘straightway’ — ‘where Shiloh is, there shall the gathering of the people be’ — and that we must ‘not lose time’ in improving such opportunities. The shared, dominant note is that Christ, returned to his Capernaum headquarters, is thronged by those hungry to hear him, the stage being set for a healing in which he will forgive before he cures, and so reveal his power over both soul and body. Theophylact adds that the Pharisees thought it harder to heal the body than the soul, since the body’s cure is open to view, so that Christ, doing first what they reckoned the easier thing, proves he can do both; Pseudo-Chrysostom notes the faith Jesus saw was the bearers’ as well as the sick man’s, who uncovered the roof when the door was blocked. The witnesses agree the healing is set to display Christ’s power over soul and body alike. Henry adds that one invited another — ‘Come, let us go see Jesus’ — so that his house could not contain the visitants who pressed in to hear.
2:
‘And straightway many were gathered together, insomuch that there was no room to receive them, no, not so much as about the door: and he preached the word unto them.’ The few witnesses, chiefly grammatical-historical, note the press of the crowd and the priority of the word.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on Mark’s graphic detail. Robertson calls it ‘another graphic Markan detail seen through Peter’s eyes,’ the double negative intensifying the picture — the house packed inside and a jam outside the door that opened onto the street. Barnes and Ellicott explain ‘preached the word’ (literally ‘spake the word’) as the doctrine of the kingdom, the ‘word of God’ (compare Acts 6:2-7); Robertson notes Mark’s favourite descriptive imperfect (elalei), and that the verb, though it can mean mere chatter, here denotes ‘the most serious kind of speech.’

The Wesleyan Wesley observes that ‘hitherto continued the general impression on their hearts’ — even at Capernaum ‘all who heard received the word with joy’ — before the scribes’ opposition began. Gill notes the people came ‘from all parts of the city.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: such was the eagerness to hear Christ that the house and even the doorway were choked with hearers, and into that crowded space he was preaching the word. The detail, the grammatical commentators observe, bears the stamp of an eyewitness; and it sets the scene for the paralytic’s friends, who, finding no way in, will resort to the roof — the press of the multitude being the very obstacle that calls forth their remarkable faith (v.4). Robertson notes the verb for Christ’s speaking (laleo), though it can mean mere chatter, here marks the most serious speech, and Mark’s descriptive imperfect pictures him steadily preaching the word; the witnesses agree the eager press that filled house and doorway is the very obstacle that will call forth the bearers’ faith in the verses that follow. Wesley observes that hitherto, even at Capernaum, all who heard received the word with joy, before the scribes’ opposition began to raise prejudice against him.
3:
‘And they come unto him, bringing one sick of the palsy, which was borne of four.’ The few witnesses, grammatical-historical with Wesley, note the vivid Markan particulars and the helplessness of the paralytic.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the detail. Robertson calls ‘and they come’ a ‘fine illustration of Mark’s vivid dramatic historical present,’ preserved by Luke (Luke 5:18) but not Matthew, and ‘borne by four’ ‘another picturesque Markan detail not in the others.’ Clarke notes the man ‘appears to have been too feeble to come himself, and too weak to be carried in any other way,’ one bearer at each corner of the couch. Ellicott observes the number of bearers is given by Mark only.

Barnes refers to the parallel in Matthew 9:2, and Gill notes the company were ‘townsmen, friends, and relations’ of the sick man.

With so brief and harmonious a body of comment the synthesis is necessarily short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: a man wholly disabled by palsy is brought to Christ on a pallet by four who carry him. The detail, the grammatical commentators stress, is one of the vivid touches that mark Mark’s Gospel as drawn from Peter’s recollection; and the helplessness of the paralytic, unable to come or even be carried by one, throws into relief both the labour of love in his friends and the faith that will not be deterred by the crowd — a faith Christ is about to honour in forgiving and healing him. Robertson notes that Luke preserves Mark’s vivid present ‘they come’ where Matthew has the imperfect, and Clarke that the man was too weak to be carried by fewer than four; the witnesses agree the helplessness of the paralytic sets in relief both the labour of love in his friends and the faith Christ is about to honour with pardon and healing. Ellicott notes that the number of the bearers is given by Mark alone, one of the vivid touches that mark his Gospel as drawn from the recollection of Peter. Wesley simply refers to the parallels in Matthew and Luke, and the witnesses agree that the man, unable to come or even be carried by one, was wholly dependent on the love and faith of those who bore him to Christ.
4:
‘And when they could not come nigh unto him for the press, they uncovered the roof where he was… and let down the bed.’ The witnesses dwell on the eastern flat roof and on the determined faith that broke through it.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the architecture. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson describe the flat roofs reached by an outside stair, and the bearers climbing up to dig out (exoruxantes) the tiles, laths, and hardened clay over the place where Jesus was (compare Luke 5:19, ‘through the tiles’). Ellicott notes Mark’s strong expressions of the damage done, and that the ‘bed’ is the grabatus, the poor man’s pallet (so John 5:8, Acts 9:33). Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic notice of ‘the way of the roof’ for removing a bier.

Robertson observes it is ‘not clear where Jesus was’ — downstairs, upstairs, or in the quadrangle — but the friends found a way over the standing crowd. Wesley notes they ‘broke the passage wider’ when the trap-door was not wide enough.

The shared, dominant note is that the four bearers, barred by the crowd from the door, would not be turned back but tore open the roof to lower their friend before Christ — a persistence the next verse names as ‘their faith.’ The witnesses see in the labour and ingenuity of these men the very pattern of importunate faith that presses through every obstacle to bring a helpless soul to the Saviour, refusing to let the press of difficulties keep them from him who alone could heal both body and soul. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic discussion of removing a bier by ‘the way of the roof,’ and Robertson grants it is unclear whether Jesus was below, above, or in the courtyard; Wesley notes they broke the passage wider when the trap-door proved too narrow. The witnesses agree the friends’ ingenuity in tearing open the roof is the very pattern of importunate faith pressing through every obstacle to bring a helpless soul to Christ. Ellicott notes Mark’s strong expressions of the damage done to the roof, and that the ‘bed’ is the poor man’s pallet, the same word found in John 5 and Acts; the witnesses agree the friends’ persistence is the very pattern of importunate faith.
5:
‘When Jesus saw their faith, he said unto the sick of the palsy, Son, thy sins be forgiven thee.’ The few witnesses agree the faith was that of all five, and that Christ’s first gift was the astonishing forgiveness of sins.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress whose faith was seen. Robertson holds it was ‘the faith of the four men and of the man himself,’ for there is ‘no reason for excluding his faith’; all had confidence in Christ’s power and willingness. He notes the ‘astonishing thing… is that Jesus forgave his sins instead of healing him,’ the sins having ‘probably caused the paralysis.’ Barnes explains ‘Son’ (literally ‘child’) as ‘a word of consolation… an endearing appellation’ to inspire confidence (compare Ephesians 2:8).

The Reformed Gill marks the faith of both the sick man and his friends — the one in suffering himself to be brought ‘under so much weakness,’ the others in ‘breaking through so many difficulties to get him to Christ.’

The fathers (carried over from the previous verses) read the order as deliberate: Christ ‘first healed by the remission of sins that which he had come to seek, that is, a soul’ (Chrysostom). The shared, dominant note is that Christ, seeing a faith expressed in the very labour of bringing the man, met it not first with bodily cure but with the deeper gift of pardon. The witnesses agree this both reveals the root of the man’s misery in his sin and sets up the great question of the verses that follow: by what authority does Jesus forgive — a prerogative the scribes rightly know belongs to God alone. Robertson notes the sins had probably caused the paralysis, so that the forgiveness reaches the root of the man’s misery, and that ‘are forgiven’ is the aoristic present of punctiliar action; the witnesses agree Christ’s first gift of pardon, before any bodily cure, both reveals the cause of his suffering and sets up the scribes’ charge and Christ’s proof of his authority to forgive. Gill marks the faith of both the sick man, in suffering himself to be brought under so much weakness, and the friends, in breaking through every difficulty to reach Christ.
6:
‘But there were certain of the scribes sitting there, and reasoning in their hearts.’ The few witnesses agree these scribes had come to find fault, and that Christ read the silent murmur of their hearts.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe them. Robertson calls it ‘another of Mark’s pictures through Peter’s eyes’: the scribes (and Pharisees, Luke 5:21) were there ‘to cause trouble, to pick flaws,’ their jealousy roused by Christ’s popularity and power; though they spoke no word aloud, ‘their looks gave them away,’ and Jesus ‘at once recognises the hostile atmosphere’ (v.8). Ellicott notes Luke’s detail that they had come ‘from every village of Galilee, and Judea, and Jerusalem’ (Luke 5:17).

The Wesleyan Wesley makes the pointed observation: ‘see whence the first offence cometh!’ — not from the plain, unlettered people, who ‘rejoiced in the light, till these men of learning came, to put darkness for light’; he cries ‘woe to all such blind guides!’ Gill notes they were ‘sitting… to watch and observe what he said and did.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: amid a believing and rejoicing crowd sat the learned critics, silently reasoning that Christ had blasphemed. Their objection, the grammatical commentators note, was never spoken, yet Jesus perceived it in his spirit; and the Wesleyan observation stands — that the first hostility to Christ came not from the simple hearers but from the religious experts, whose jealousy turned the gift of pardon into a charge of blasphemy, and who thus, by their own reasoning, set the stage for Christ to prove his right to forgive. Robertson notes their debate ‘was written on their faces’ though no sound came, and Ellicott that they had gathered from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem (Luke 5:17); the witnesses agree the first hostility came from the learned critics, whose silent reasoning Christ would at once expose and answer. Wesley adds the pointed observation that the first offence came not from the plain, unlettered people, who rejoiced in the light, but from the learned, whom he calls blind guides.
7:
‘Why doth this man thus speak blasphemies? who can forgive sins but God only?’ The witnesses agree the scribes’ logic was sound — only God forgives sins — its one flaw being their refusal to see that Christ held that very relation to God.

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the point. Robertson notes the scribes ‘justify the charge with the conviction that God alone has the power to forgive sins’: ‘their logic was correct. The only flaw in it was the possibility that Jesus held a peculiar relation to God which justified his claim. So the two forces clash here as now on the deity of Christ.’ Barnes points to the prophetic claim that God blots out transgressions (Isaiah 43:25, Daniel 9:9). Ellicott gives the better text, ‘He blasphemeth.’

The Reformed Gill explains they ‘took Christ to be a mere man,’ and so reasoned he must be a blasphemer in assuming what was peculiar to God. Lightfoot supplies a rabbinic dispute confirming that the forgiveness of sins was held to belong to God alone.

The shared, dominant note is that the scribes correctly grasped the magnitude of Christ’s words: to forgive sins is a divine prerogative, and for a mere man to claim it would indeed be blasphemy. Their error, the witnesses agree, lay not in their premise but in their conclusion — they would not allow that the one before them was more than man. The whole episode thus turns, as Robertson observes, on the deity of Christ: by claiming and proving the power to forgive, Jesus answers their charge not by denying their theology but by demonstrating that he is the God against whom they suppose he has blasphemed. Robertson observes that ‘the two forces clash here as now on the deity of Christ,’ the scribes’ premise being sound but their conclusion false; Lightfoot cites the rabbinic dispute that even a heavenly messenger could not forgive sins, that prerogative belonging to God alone. The witnesses agree the whole episode turns on whether Jesus is more than man. Barnes points to the prophetic claim that God alone blots out transgressions, so that the scribes rightly held the forgiveness of sins to be a divine prerogative.
8:
‘And immediately when Jesus perceived in his spirit that they so reasoned within themselves.’ The two witnesses agree this perception of unspoken thoughts is a mark both of Christ’s true humanity and of his divine knowledge.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that ‘the special mention of the spirit as the region of our Lord’s consciousness is… peculiar to St. Mark,’ and is ‘not without importance in its bearing on the reality and completeness of our Lord’s human nature.’ The Reformed Gill, by contrast, refers the knowledge to Christ’s ‘divine nature, in and by which he knew all things, even the most sacred thoughts of men’s hearts’: as soon as the scribes conceived their murmur, ‘they were perceived by him, and told to them.’

With only two voices the synthesis is brief, but they together hold both truths in balance. On the one side, Mark’s phrase ‘in his spirit’ locates the perception within the genuine human consciousness of Jesus, witnessing to the reality of his manhood; on the other, the very fact that he read thoughts no lips had uttered witnesses to a knowledge proper to God, who alone searches the heart.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ instantly knew the scribes’ secret reasoning and brought it into the open. This discernment, the witnesses agree, is itself an answer to their charge: the one whom they silently accuse of blasphemy proves, by reading the unspoken thoughts of their hearts, that he possesses the divine knowledge which, with the power to forgive sins he is about to demonstrate, marks him out as more than the mere man they take him to be — so that his very perception of their objection begins to refute it. Ellicott stresses that ‘in his spirit’ bears on the reality of Christ’s human nature, while Gill refers the knowledge to his divine nature that searches all hearts; the witnesses hold both truths, that the perception of unspoken thoughts witnesses at once to true manhood and to a knowledge proper to God, who alone searches the heart. The two voices together hold the balance, that the reading of unspoken thoughts proves Christ both truly man and possessed of a knowledge that belongs to God alone.
9:
‘Whether is it easier to say… Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Arise, and take up thy bed, and walk?’ The few witnesses explain the force of the question, by which Christ proves the unseen by the seen.

The Reformed Gill explains the question was put ‘to prove his deity, and clear himself from the charge of blasphemy’: he who could cure the palsy by a word ‘had power to forgive… sins.’ The grammatical-historical Lightfoot clarifies the idiom — ‘easier’ means the plainer, more obvious thing: ‘it is easier to say to the paralytic, Thy sins are forgiven thee’ (a claim none could test) than to say ‘Arise and walk’ (a claim at once open to proof) — so Christ chooses the testable word to vindicate the other.

The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem, on point, names Jesus ‘the Healer… physician of souls and bodies’: ‘since the body was palsied for the sin of the soul, he ministered first to the soul that he might extend the healing to the body’ (compare ‘Help thou mine unbelief’).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ’s question is a masterstroke of argument. To pronounce forgiveness is easy in the sense that none can disprove it; to command a paralytic to rise and walk is to stake the claim on a result all can see. By choosing to do the visible, verifiable thing, Jesus furnishes the proof of the invisible, unverifiable one: the power that heals the body openly attests the authority that forgives the soul in secret, so that the healing becomes the warrant of the pardon and the answer to the scribes’ charge of blasphemy. Lightfoot explains the question concerns not the ease of pronouncing the words but the plainness of the sense — forgiveness none can test against healing all can verify; Cyril of Jerusalem names Jesus the Healer who ministers first to the soul. The witnesses agree Christ stakes the unseen pardon on the seen miracle, choosing the testable word to vindicate the other. Gill adds that the question was put to prove his deity and clear him of the charge of blasphemy, since he who could cure the palsy by a word had power to forgive sins.
10:
‘But that ye may know that the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins.’ The witnesses agree this is the heart of the episode: Christ works the visible miracle to prove his authority to forgive.

The grammatical-historical Robertson draws it out fully: Jesus ‘chose the harder first, the forgiveness which they could not see,’ and now performs the healing ‘which all could see, that all could know’ that the Son of Man — his favourite self-designation and ‘a claim to be the Messiah’ — ‘really had the authority and power to forgive sins, here and now, without waiting for the day of judgment.’ He notes the remarkable parenthesis (‘he saith to the sick of the palsy’) appears identically in all three Synoptics, proof that Matthew and Luke followed Mark. Barnes ties the ‘power on earth’ to Acts 5:31.

The Reformed Gill notes that, though by his mean appearance they thought him a mere man, Christ affirms his authority as the true Messiah, ‘the Son of man,’ a title used of him in the Old Testament (compare Daniel 7:13).

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem here is a Passion-meditation, tangential to the verse. The shared, dominant note is that Christ deliberately grounds the unseen gift of pardon on the seen miracle of healing: the very deed the scribes could witness becomes the demonstration of the authority they had questioned. By calling himself the Son of Man with power on earth to forgive sins, Jesus claims a present, divine prerogative and proves it before their eyes, so that the healing of the body is, as the older writers saw, the public seal upon his right to absolve the soul. Robertson notes the identical parenthesis (‘he saith to the sick of the palsy’) in all three Synoptics proves Matthew and Luke followed Mark, and that Jesus claims authority to forgive ‘here and now, without waiting for the day of judgment’; Gill grounds ‘Son of man’ in its Old Testament use. The witnesses agree the visible deed is the public seal upon Christ’s right to absolve the soul.
11:
‘I say unto thee, Arise, and take up thy bed, and go thy way into thine house.’ The two witnesses agree this authoritative word, openly fulfilled, is the visible proof of Christ’s power to forgive.

The Reformed Gill notes that Christ bid the man ‘in an authoritative way’ to rise from the bed in which he was brought, take it up upon his shoulders ‘directly, and in the face of all the people,’ and carry it home. The command was public and immediate, that all might witness the cure.

The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem, on point, sets the word in its place: Jesus, ‘the Healer… physician of souls and bodies,’ says to the palsied, ‘Sin no more’ and ‘Take up thy bed and walk,’ for ‘since the body was palsied for the sin of the soul, he ministered first to the soul that he might extend the healing to the body.’ He bids the doubting say, ‘Help thou mine unbelief,’ and the ailing draw near in faith.

With only two voices the synthesis is brief, but they agree on the substance: the command to the paralytic is the open counterpart to the secret word of pardon. What Christ had done invisibly in forgiving the man’s sins he now does visibly in restoring his limbs, and the two are bound together — the body healed because the soul is forgiven, the cure of the one the proof of the other. The witnesses see in ‘arise… and go’ the effectual word of the Lord of both soul and body, who not only declares the man pardoned but raises him up before the eyes of all, that none might doubt the authority by which he had spoken. Gill stresses the command was public and immediate, that all might witness the cure; Cyril of Jerusalem joins ‘Sin no more’ with ‘Take up thy bed,’ since the body was palsied for the soul’s sin. The witnesses agree the open command is the visible counterpart of the secret word of pardon, Christ showing himself Lord of both soul and body. The witnesses agree the effectual word of the Lord both declared the man pardoned and raised him up before the eyes of all, that none might doubt the authority by which he spoke.
12:
‘And immediately he arose, took up the bed, and went forth before them all; insomuch that they were all amazed, and glorified God.’ The witnesses agree the instant, complete cure silenced the scribes and moved the crowd to wonder and praise.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the effect. Robertson notes the man ‘picked up his pallet and walked… before them all,’ an ‘amazing proceeding’ that made further refutation of the scribes unnecessary; Jesus ‘had acted with the power of God and claimed equality with God and had made good his claim.’ Lightfoot pictures the healed man, now ‘strong and lusty,’ pressing through the very crowd that had barred his entrance. Barnes gives the people’s cry, ‘We never saw it on this fashion’ (compare John 7:31).

The Wesleyan Clarke turns it to a meditation: as the palsy is the soul’s inability to rise or walk in God’s ways, so ‘grace can repair all in a moment’ by the almighty will of God; Wesley notes ‘they were all amazed, even the scribes themselves for a time.’

The shared, dominant note is that the immediate and public healing crowned the whole episode: the man who had been carried in helpless walked out carrying his bed, and the amazement was too great for words but for the confession that they had never seen the like. The witnesses agree the miracle settled the question the scribes had raised — Christ had openly proved his authority to forgive by openly healing — and that the right response, shown by the wondering crowd, was to glorify God; the cure of the body being, as the older writers saw, a parable of the grace that can in a moment raise the palsied soul to walk in the ways of God. Robertson notes the amazement was too great for words but for ‘We never saw it on this fashion,’ and Lightfoot pictures the healed man, now strong and lusty, pressing through the very crowd that had barred his entrance; the witnesses agree the public cure settled the scribes’ question and moved all to glorify God. Clarke turns it to a meditation, that as the palsy is the soul’s helplessness, so grace can repair all in a moment by the almighty will of God who commands and does whatever he commands.
13:
‘And he went forth again by the sea side; and all the multitude resorted unto him, and he taught them.’ The witnesses note Christ’s preaching by the sea and his call of Levi, the publican who became Matthew.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Barnes and Robertson place it by the sea of Tiberias near Capernaum (Mark 1:16); Robertson pictures Jesus getting ‘a whiff of fresh air by the sea,’ yet soon thronged again, ‘teaching them’ (descriptive imperfect). They note the identity of Levi with Matthew the Evangelist (Matthew 9:9).

The fathers draw out the call. Bede observes that ‘the same person is called Levi and Matthew,’ Mark and Luke sparing the Evangelist’s old name out of reverence while Matthew ‘is a just accuser of himself,’ that ‘no one who is converted should despair… since he himself was suddenly changed from a publican into an Apostle.’ Theophylact notes that the more one flees glory the more she pursues, and Pseudo-Jerome reads Levi (‘Appointed’) following the Word who says ‘he who doth not quit all… cannot be my disciple’ (Luke 14:33).

The Puritan Henry observes Christ ‘had a strong voice’ and went to the seaside for room, and that wherever his doctrine is preached ‘though it be driven into corners… we must follow it.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ, teaching the thronging multitude by the sea, there called a despised tax-gatherer to be his disciple — a proof, the witnesses agree, that none converted need despair of grace, since the publican Levi was in a moment made an apostle and Evangelist. Robertson pictures Jesus seeking fresh air by the sea yet thronged again and teaching them, and Chrysostom notes the same man bears two names, as others in Scripture do; the witnesses agree that, teaching the crowd by the shore, Christ there called the publican Levi, a proof that none converted should despair of grace. Henry adds that wherever Christ’s doctrine is faithfully preached, though it be driven into corners or to the seaside, we must follow it and not lose the opportunity.
14:
‘And as he passed by, he saw Levi the son of Alphaeus sitting at the receipt of custom, and said unto him, Follow me. And he arose and followed him.’ The few witnesses agree on the identity of Levi and the promptness of his obedience.

The grammatical-historical commentators establish the man. Barnes, Clarke and Robertson identify Levi as Matthew the Evangelist, it being common among the Jews to bear two names, sitting at the toll-gate (telonion) where he collected toll for Herod Antipas (compare Matthew 9:9). Robertson stresses the suddenness and sharpness of the call and the readiness of the response: ‘great decisions are often made on a moment’s notice,’ Levi ‘a fine object lesson for business men who put off service to Christ to carry on their business.’

The Wesleyan Clarke notes Levi was ‘a Jew, though employed in the odious office of a tax-gatherer,’ and Gill that the call came as Christ went from Capernaum toward the sea.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: at a single word from Christ, a despised publican left his lucrative post and followed him. The grammatical commentators see in the abruptness of the summons and the immediacy of the obedience the pattern of true discipleship — Levi rose at once, abandoning the receipt of custom for the service of Christ. The witnesses agree that this call of a tax-gatherer, like the forgiving of the paralytic, displays the grace that reaches the despised and the lost, and prepares for the feast at which Christ will defend his eating with publicans and sinners (v.15). Robertson presses that ‘great decisions are often made on a moment’s notice,’ Levi a lesson for those who put off Christ for business, while Clarke notes the publican was a Jew in an odious office; the witnesses agree the abrupt summons and immediate obedience are the very pattern of discipleship, the despised tax-gatherer leaving all to follow. Gill notes the call came as Christ went from Capernaum toward the sea, and the witnesses agree that this reaching of the despised, like the forgiving of the paralytic, displays the grace that seeks the lost.
15:
‘And it came to pass, that, as Jesus sat at meat in his house, many publicans and sinners sat also together with Jesus and his disciples.’ The witnesses agree Levi made a feast in gratitude, gathering his old associates to meet Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the gathering. Barnes explains the feast was made by Matthew ‘in honour of the Saviour’ (Luke 5:29), the ‘sinners’ being ‘sinners of abandoned character… fit companions of publicans’; and that the ‘many’ who followed gave proof that ‘in the early part of his ministry’ Christ was ‘extremely popular.’ Gill notes the entertainment was in Levi’s house in Capernaum, ‘in token of gratitude.’

The Wesleyan Wesley sees the publicans and sinners partly invited by Matthew, ‘moved with compassion for his old companions in sin,’ but the greater part pressing in unbidden, ‘encouraged by his gracious words and the tenderness of his behaviour, and impatient to hear more.’

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem here concerns the naming of the disciples ‘Christians’ at Antioch (Acts 11:26), and so does not bear on the verse. The shared, dominant note is that the newly-called publican opened his house to Christ and to the very people his old trade had associated him with, so that the Saviour sat at table with a ‘motley crew’ of publicans and sinners. The witnesses see in this both the gratitude of the converted, who longs to bring his former companions to Christ, and the condescension of the Saviour, who freely receives the outcast — a scene that at once provokes the Pharisees’ murmur and displays the very heart of his mission to the lost. Barnes infers from the ‘many’ who followed that Christ was extremely popular early in his ministry, before the rich and great turned against him; Wesley sees Matthew moved with compassion for his old companions in sin. The witnesses agree the converted publican opened his house that the Saviour might freely receive the outcast. Gill notes the entertainment was in Levi’s house in Capernaum, made in token of gratitude for the high favour bestowed on him in his calling.
16:
‘How is it that he eateth and drinketh with publicans and sinners?’ The witnesses agree the scribes of the Pharisees took offence at Christ’s table-fellowship with the outcast.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the objection. Robertson gives the correct text, ‘the scribes of the Pharisees,’ who, invited or standing by to ‘pick flaws,’ were scandalised at ‘a motley crew’ following Jesus; for ‘it was an offence for a Jew to eat with Gentiles’ (compare Acts 11:3), and publicans and sinners were ‘regarded like Gentiles.’ Lightfoot lists the rabbinic catalogue of ‘sinners’ — dicers, usurers, plunderers, publicans, shepherds. Clarke notes a textual variant, ‘Why doth your Master eat?’

The Wesleyan Wesley observes that ‘the wise men being joined by the saints of the world went a little farther in raising prejudices’ against Christ, and that in his answer he uses ‘no harshness, but only calm, dispassionate reasoning.’ Gill notes they were offended that he did not fast and especially that he ate with such company.

The shared, dominant note is that the scribes and Pharisees, jealous and censorious, made Christ’s very mercy a reproach: that he should eat with publicans and sinners seemed to them a defilement. The witnesses agree the objection sprang from a self-righteous separatism that despised the outcast and resented the grace shown them; and that it set up the great saying of the next verse, in which Christ defends his table-fellowship not by denying that these were sinners but by declaring that it is precisely the sick who need the physician, and sinners whom he came to call. Robertson notes it was an offence for a Jew to eat with those reckoned as Gentiles, a scruple some early Jewish Christians still felt (Acts 11:3), and Lightfoot lists the rabbinic ‘sinners’ — dicers, usurers, publicans, shepherds; the witnesses agree the murmur sprang from a self-righteous separatism that resented the grace shown to the outcast. Wesley observes that in his answer Christ uses no harshness but only calm, dispassionate reasoning, meeting the Pharisees’ censoriousness with the figure of the physician. Gill notes the scribes and Pharisees were offended both that he did not fast as they did and especially that he ate with such company, making his very mercy a reproach against him.
17:
‘They that are whole have no need of the physician, but they that are sick: I came not to call the righteous, but sinners.’ The witnesses agree this is Christ’s great defence of his mission to the lost, and note a textual point.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the saying. Robertson notes Jesus ‘for the sake of argument accepts the claim of the Pharisees to be righteous,’ though elsewhere he exposes them (Matthew 23); the ‘whole’ or strong ‘are not those who need the physician in an epidemic.’ Robertson, Clarke and Ellicott observe that the words ‘to repentance’ are not genuine in Mark (though genuine in Luke 5:32); Clarke adds that ‘properly speaking, the righteous cannot be called to repentance.’ Ellicott notes Mark, writing for Gentiles, omits the citation of Hosea 6:6 found in Matthew.

The Wesleyan Wesley puts it sharply: ‘if these were righteous I should not call them; but now, they are the very persons I came to save.’ Barnes points to the parallels and to Christ’s coming to seek the lost (1 Timothy 1:15).

The shared, dominant note is that Christ answers the murmur with the figure of the physician: as the doctor goes to the sick, not the healthy, so he came to call sinners, not those who reckon themselves righteous. The witnesses agree the saying both justifies his table-fellowship with publicans and gently rebukes the Pharisees’ self-righteousness — for those who think themselves whole feel no need of him, while it is the sin-sick who flock to the great Physician. The very company that scandalised the scribes was, on Christ’s own showing, the proper object of his saving errand. Robertson notes Jesus accepts for argument the Pharisees’ claim to righteousness, though they fell far short, and that ‘the well need not the physician in an epidemic’; Clarke and Ellicott observe ‘to repentance’ is wanting in Mark though genuine in Luke. The witnesses agree the physician-figure both justifies his table-fellowship and rebukes Pharisaic self-righteousness. Barnes points to Christ’s coming to seek and save the lost (1 Timothy 1:15), the very company that scandalised the scribes being the proper object of his saving errand.
18:
‘Why do the disciples of John and of the Pharisees fast, but thy disciples fast not?’ The witnesses agree the question, raised on a fast-day, gives Christ occasion to unfold the newness of his dispensation under the figure of the bridegroom.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Robertson notes Levi’s feast probably fell on a weekly fast-day, so ‘there was a clash of standpoints’; the disciples of John, more ascetic (Matthew 11:18), here ‘line up with the Pharisees against Jesus,’ though there was ‘no real congeniality’ between John’s asceticism and Pharisaic formalism. Barnes and Clarke note the Pharisees fasted twice a week (Luke 18:12).

The fathers read it of the new covenant. Theophylact and Bede hold that John’s disciples, ‘in an imperfect state, continued in Jewish customs,’ while Christ the Bridegroom brings ‘not sorrow… but rest’; the sons of the bride-chamber are the apostles, made partakers of every joy, who fast not while the Bridegroom is present. Augustine and Pseudo-Chrysostom expound the bridegroom ‘taken away,’ when they shall fast in longing for his coming.

The Puritan Henry observes that strict professors are apt ‘to make their own practice a standard’ and condemn all who fall short. The shared, dominant note is that the disciples’ not fasting was no laxity but the fitness of the hour: while the Bridegroom is present, his friends keep festival, not mourning; fasting will have its season when he is taken away. Christ thus answers the charge by declaring that his coming has brought a new and joyful order, which the old forms of mere ritual fasting can no longer measure. Robertson notes there was no real congeniality between John’s asceticism and Pharisaic formalism, the Pharisees having hated John, and Henry that strict professors make their own practice a standard and condemn all who fall short; the witnesses agree the disciples’ not fasting was the fitness of the hour, the Bridegroom being present among them. Bede adds that he who is incorporated into the members of Christ by faithful love cannot fast, because he feasts upon the Bread of life, while the imperfect cleave to the old Jewish customs.
19:
‘Can the children of the bridechamber fast, while the bridegroom is with them?’ The witnesses agree Christ adopts the figure of the wedding to show that his presence is a time for joy, not mourning.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the image. Robertson notes the ‘sons of the bridechamber’ are not merely the groomsmen but the guests, and that Jesus ‘adopts the Baptist’s own metaphor’ (John 3:29), identifying himself with the bridegroom of the Old Testament — God in covenant with Israel (Hosea 2:19) — so that ‘mourning does not suit the wedding feast.’ Barnes ties it to the marriage figure of Matthew 25:1; Clarke adds an eastern parallel of wedding mirth turned to sorrow at the bridegroom’s departure.

The Antiochene Cyril of Jerusalem, loosely on point, recalls that he who at Cana turned water into wine for ‘the children of the bride-chamber’ would much more bestow the fruition of his body and blood. Gill notes the answer was directed to both John’s disciples and the Pharisees.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s presence makes his disciples wedding-guests, for whom fasting would be as unfitting as mourning at a marriage. The witnesses see in his taking the bridegroom-figure a quiet but profound claim: he is the divine Bridegroom of prophecy, and the days of his earthly company are days of festal joy. The disciples’ not fasting is therefore no fault but the very fitness of the season; the forms of sorrow belong to a later day, when the Bridegroom shall be taken from them, and not to the bright hour of his presence among them. Robertson notes Jesus identifies himself with the bridegroom of the Old Testament, God in covenant with Israel (Hosea 2:19), so that mourning unfits the wedding; Cyril of Jerusalem recalls the wine of Cana given the children of the bride-chamber. The witnesses agree Christ’s presence makes his disciples wedding-guests, for whom fasting would be as unfitting as mourning at a marriage. Gill notes the answer was directed to both John’s disciples and the Pharisees, and the witnesses agree the days of his earthly company are days of festal joy, not of sorrow.
20:
‘But the days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken away from them, and then shall they fast in those days.’ The few witnesses agree this foretells the season of fasting after Christ’s departure.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the prediction. Barnes points forward to the apostles’ fasting after the ascension (Acts 13:2); Clarke records a textual variant, ‘in that day’ for ‘in those days,’ referring to the day when Christ should be delivered up. Gill observes the words were ‘in some sense now come’ to John’s disciples, their master being imprisoned, so that ‘it was a mourning time with them.’

With only three brief voices the synthesis is short, but they agree on the substance: Christ foretells that his bodily absence will be the time for his disciples to fast. The figure of the bridegroom, begun in the previous verse, is here completed — the wedding-joy of his presence will give place to the sorrow of his being ‘taken away,’ a word the grammatical commentators see pointing to his death and ascension.

The shared, dominant note is that fasting has its proper season: not while the Bridegroom is present, but when he is withdrawn. The witnesses agree Christ does not abolish fasting but reorders it, binding it to the heart’s longing for an absent Lord rather than to the mechanical observance of fixed days. The apostles’ later fasting, undertaken in such longing and in the work of the gospel, is the fulfilment of this word; and the church, between the ascension and the return of her Lord, keeps her fasts as the expression of a love that waits for the Bridegroom’s coming again. Barnes points to the apostles’ fasting after the ascension (Acts 13:2), and Clarke records the variant ‘in that day’ for the day Christ was delivered up; Gill notes the words were already in part fulfilled for John’s mourning disciples. The witnesses agree fasting has its season — not while the Bridegroom is present, but when he is withdrawn. The witnesses agree Christ does not abolish fasting but reorders it, binding it to the heart’s longing for an absent Lord rather than to the mechanical observance of fixed days.
21:
‘No man seweth a piece of new cloth on an old garment.’ The few witnesses agree the parable shows that Christ’s new dispensation cannot be patched onto the old forms.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the figure. Robertson notes the rare verb ‘seweth on’ (epiraptei, only here in the New Testament), and that Matthew and Luke use a different word for clapping on a patch (Matthew 9:16, Luke 5:36); the ‘new’ cloth is unfulled, unshrunk, raw cloth that would tear the old. Barnes glosses it as ‘raw, or unwrought’ cloth.

The Reformed Gill reads the ‘old garment’ as ‘the traditions of the elders,’ particularly concerning eating and fasting, which were ‘new things in comparison of the commands of God,’ many devised in that age; the gospel is not to be stitched as a patch onto such worn-out human additions.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ teaches by a homely image that his new way is incompatible with the old ritual forms. To sew raw, unshrunk cloth onto a worn garment is to make the rent worse, for the new will shrink and tear the old. So the freshness of the gospel cannot be confined within the forms of Pharisaic tradition or made a mere addition to them; it requires, as the next verse will add under the figure of the wineskins, a new vessel altogether. The witnesses see in the parable Christ’s answer to the fasting question carried to its principle: his coming is not a repair of the old order but the inauguration of a new. Robertson notes the rare verb ‘seweth on’ (only here in the New Testament) and that the new cloth is raw and unshrunk, while Gill reads the old garment as the traditions of the elders; the witnesses agree the gospel cannot be patched onto the worn forms of the old order, for the new would only tear the old the worse. The witnesses see in the paired parables of the garment and the wineskins a single lesson: Christ has come not to mend or fill the old, but to bring a new dispensation received in a new spirit.
22:
‘And no man putteth new wine into old bottles.’ The few witnesses agree the second parable, like the first, teaches that the gospel requires new vessels, not the worn forms of the old order.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the image. The ‘bottles’ are skins; new, fermenting wine would burst old, brittle skins, so ‘new wine’ must go ‘into fresh wineskins’ (compare Matthew 9:17, Luke 5:38). Robertson notes the closing clause, though bracketed by some editors here, is genuine in Luke and may be so in Mark.

The Reformed Gill reads the ‘old bottles’ as the Scribes and Pharisees, the self-satisfied ‘whole’ whom Christ came not to call, and the ‘new wine’ as the love of God, the blessings of the new covenant, and the gospel itself — which ‘is not received by carnal men’ whose old vessels cannot contain it.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the fermenting power of the new wine demands a new and supple skin, for the old, hardened skins would burst and the wine be spilled. So the living power of the gospel cannot be poured into the rigid, outworn forms of Pharisaic ritual without ruin to both. The witnesses see in the paired parables of the garment and the wineskins a single lesson: Christ has come not to mend or fill the old, but to bring a new dispensation that must be received in a new spirit. The forms of mere ceremonial fasting belong to the old skins; the joy and freedom of the kingdom call for the new. Robertson notes the closing clause, though bracketed by some editors, is genuine in Luke and may be so in Mark, and Gill reads the new wine as the love of God and the blessings of the new covenant, which carnal men cannot contain; the witnesses agree the living power of the gospel demands new vessels, not the rigid forms of Pharisaic ritual. The witnesses agree the forms of mere ceremonial fasting belong to the old skins, while the joy and freedom of the kingdom call for the new, received in a new and supple spirit.
23:
‘And his disciples began… to pluck the ears of corn’ on the sabbath. The witnesses agree the disciples acted from hunger and lawfully, and that Christ defends them against the Pharisees’ charge of sabbath-breaking.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the facts. Barnes and Robertson explain ‘corn’ means grain (wheat or barley), which the hungry disciples plucked and rubbed in their hands, as the Law allowed (Deuteronomy 23:25); the offence alleged was not theft but the ‘work’ of preparing food on the sabbath. Barnes draws the lesson that ‘poverty is not disgraceful,’ Christ suffering his own to be poor.

The fathers read it of the new freedom. Pseudo-Chrysostom holds the disciples, ‘freed from the figure and united to the truth, do not keep the figurative feast of the sabbath’; Augustine notes the Law allowed a passer-by to pluck and eat another’s corn, so the charge was of sabbath-breaking, not stealing. Bede and Theophylact recount David and the showbread by way of precedent.

The Puritan Henry sums the principle: ‘the sabbath is a sacred and Divine institution; a privilege and benefit, not a task and drudgery,’ never designed ‘to be a burden.’ The shared, dominant note is that the disciples’ plucking of grain to satisfy hunger broke no divine law but only the Pharisees’ rigid gloss upon it; and that Christ, far from condemning them, will vindicate them by the example of David and the great principle that the sabbath was made for man. The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note on Antioch is here tangential to the verse. Barnes draws the lesson that poverty is not disgraceful, Christ suffering his own to be poor, and Augustine that the Law allowed a passer-by to pluck and eat another’s corn, so the charge was sabbath-breaking, not theft; the witnesses agree the disciples broke no divine law but only the Pharisees’ rigid gloss upon it. Bede and Theophylact recount David’s eating of the showbread by way of precedent, and the witnesses agree Christ will vindicate the disciples by that example and by the principle that the sabbath was made for man. Chrysostom notes the disciples ate from simple necessity of nature, not for pleasure, while the Pharisees, serving the figure and the shadow, accused them of doing wrong on the sabbath day.
24:
‘Behold, why do they on the sabbath day that which is not lawful?’ The few witnesses agree the Pharisees charged not theft but sabbath-violation, exposing the perversity of their superstition.

The grammatical-historical Barnes explains the plucking was ‘made lawful by Moses, without any distinction of days,’ but the Pharisees ‘had denied its lawfulness on the sabbath’; Christ will show, ‘from their own law, that it was not unlawful.’ Gill notes the same charge was levelled at the disciples and at Christ.

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest reading. The Pharisees blame the disciples not for taking another’s corn but for ‘violating’ the sabbath, ‘as if there had been a precept… that famishing men ought rather to die than to satisfy their hunger.’ The only end of the sabbath, Calvin urges, was that the people, free from worldly occupations, might be employed in ‘true and spiritual worship’; the Law must be interpreted ‘according to the design of the Legislator.’ The Pharisees’ rigour shows ‘the malicious and implacable nature of superstition’ joined to ‘hatred of the person.’

With so few voices the synthesis is short, but they agree on the substance: the Pharisees, watching to accuse, turned a hungry man’s harmless act into a capital breach of the sabbath. The witnesses see in their charge the very spirit of legalism — a zeal for the letter that forgets the purpose of the commandment and would sacrifice mercy to a man-made rigour. Christ’s answer, drawn from their own Scriptures and crowned by the principle that the sabbath was made for man, will expose their error and reclaim the sabbath as a gift for human good, not a snare for human conscience. Calvin urges the sabbath’s only end was that the people, free from worldly work, might engage in spiritual worship, the Law to be read by the Legislator’s design; Barnes that Christ shows from their own law the plucking was not unlawful. The witnesses agree the Pharisees’ rigour exposes the malicious nature of superstition joined to hatred of the person. Gill notes the same charge was levelled at both the disciples and Christ, and the witnesses agree the rigour sprang from a legalism that forgot the purpose of the commandment.
25:
‘Have ye never read what David did, when he had need, and was an hungred?’ The few witnesses agree Christ answers from Scripture, citing David’s eating of the showbread.

The grammatical-historical Barnes simply refers to the parallel in Matthew 12:3 and the original account (1 Samuel 21:6). Gill notes the answer was ‘a full one, and enough to silence them’: Christ meets the Pharisees on their own ground, appealing to a deed of the revered David which, by their own reckoning, broke the letter of the Law yet stood uncondemned.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem here is a meditation on the garden of Christ’s burial and the true Vine, and so does not bear on the verse.

With so brief a body of genuine comment the synthesis is short, but the substance is clear: Christ rebuts the charge of sabbath-breaking by an argument from the greater to the less. If David, in his need and hunger, lawfully ate the consecrated showbread that was reserved for the priests, how much more might the disciples, hungry on the sabbath, pluck a few ears of grain? The witnesses see in the appeal to David both an authority the Pharisees could not gainsay and a principle that necessity and mercy take precedence over ceremonial restriction. The example, drawn from their own Scriptures, prepares the way for the broader maxim Christ is about to lay down — that the sabbath was made for man, and that the Son of Man is its Lord — so that the disciples’ act, far from condemned, is shown to stand within the true intent of the Law. Gill notes the answer was full and enough to silence them, Christ meeting the Pharisees on their own ground, and Barnes refers to the parallel and to David’s deed in 1 Samuel 21:6; the witnesses agree the appeal to the revered David, who broke the letter of the Law in his need yet stood uncondemned, is an authority they could not gainsay. The witnesses agree the appeal to David, drawn from their own Scriptures, prepares the way for the broader maxim that the sabbath was made for man and the Son of Man is its Lord.
26:
‘How he… did eat the shewbread… in the days of Abiathar the high priest.’ The witnesses agree on the force of David’s precedent and grapple with the historical difficulty of the high priest’s name.

The grammatical-historical commentators face the crux. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott and Robertson note that 1 Samuel 21:1 makes Ahimelech, not Abiathar, the priest at the time; they offer that Mark names the more famous Abiathar, Ahimelech’s son and successor, who was then present and soon high priest (1 Samuel 23:6), or that both bore both names (2 Samuel 8:17), or that the Greek means simply ‘in the time of Abiathar.’ Robertson allows we may ‘leave it unexplained.’ Lightfoot adds that Abiathar was associated with the oracle by Urim and Thummim.

The Reformed Gill reads the showbread spiritually — the house of God furnishing bread for the hungry soul — and notes David came on the sabbath, when the loaves were renewed. Wesley states plainly that ‘Abimelech, the father of Abiathar, was high priest then,’ the phrase meaning ‘in the time of Abiathar, who was afterward the high priest.’

The shared, dominant note is that David’s eating of the consecrated bread, lawful only for the priests, justifies the disciples’ lesser liberty: necessity and mercy override ceremonial restriction. On the puzzle of ‘Abiathar,’ the witnesses agree it is resolved either by his prominence and near-succession, by the sharing of names between father and son, or by rendering the phrase loosely of his era — none of which touches the force of the argument. The precedent stands: if the revered David, in his need, was guiltless in setting aside a ceremonial law, the Pharisees cannot condemn the hungry disciples for a far smaller thing. Lightfoot adds that Abiathar was associated with inquiring by Urim and Thummim, so that David ate the bread given by a priest who acted by divine direction; Robertson notes the confusion in the Massoretic text and Septuagint over the two names. The witnesses agree the puzzle, however resolved, touches not the force of David’s precedent: necessity and mercy override ceremonial law. Gill reads the showbread spiritually, the house of God furnishing bread for the hungry soul, and notes that David came on the sabbath, when the loaves were renewed.
27:
‘The sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath.’ The witnesses agree this great saying, found in Mark alone, lays down the true purpose of the sabbath as a gift for man’s good.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes Mark alone has ‘this profound saying which subordinates the sabbath to man’s real welfare,’ against the rabbis’ petty rules; he cites the rabbinic parallel, ‘the sabbath is delivered unto you, and ye are not delivered unto the sabbath,’ and observes ‘Christianity has had to fight this same battle about institutionalism.’ Barnes and Clarke draw out the sabbath as a kind provision — rest from toil, time for worship and for eternity — and note that ‘where there is no sabbath, there is ignorance, vice, disorder, and crime.’

The Reformed Calvin guards the balance: those judge amiss who turn to man’s ruin the sabbath God appointed for his benefit; yet he warns against supposing the sabbath ‘entirely abolished’ — Christ ‘simply informs us what is the proper use of it.’ Wesley sums it: the sabbath ‘must give way to man’s necessity.’

The shared, dominant note is that the sabbath is a servant of human welfare, not a tyrant over it: God ordained it for man’s rest, worship, and good, so that mercy and necessity are never its violation but its true fulfilment. The witnesses agree this principle at once vindicates the hungry disciples and corrects the Pharisees’ legalism — yet, as Calvin and Barnes insist, it establishes rather than abolishes the day, reclaiming it from a burden into the benefit and blessing God designed; the right use of the sabbath being measured always by the rule of mercy and of man’s real good. Robertson notes Christianity has had to fight the same battle about institutionalism, the church itself being for man, not man for the church, and Barnes that where there is no sabbath there is ignorance, vice, and disorder; the witnesses agree the sabbath is a servant of human welfare, established not abolished, its right use measured always by the rule of mercy. Wesley sums it that the sabbath must give way to man’s necessity, and Calvin that those judge amiss who turn to man’s ruin the day God appointed for his benefit.
28:
‘Therefore the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.’ The witnesses agree this is the climax of Christ’s defence: as the Son of Man he is Lord, and so has authority over the sabbath.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the claim. Robertson notes Mark’s ‘even’ (kai), absent from the others, shows Jesus ‘knew that he was making a great claim as the Son of Man… to lordship even of the sabbath’ — ‘not the slave of the sabbath, but the master of it,’ coming ‘not to abolish, but to interpret and keep in its own place.’ He rejects the reading that makes ‘man’ in general the sabbath’s lord. Barnes ties the Son of Man’s authority to Ephesians 1:22 and Revelation 1:10.

The Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley balance the point: the Son of Man, ‘the supreme Lawgiver, hath power to dispense with his own laws’ (Wesley); yet Clarke warns against reading it of men generally, lest ‘the moral obligation of the sabbath would soon be annihilated,’ for God ordained it both as a type of rest and a means of human welfare.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a Passion-meditation, tangential to the verse. The shared, dominant note is that Christ crowns his argument with a claim of lordship: the sabbath, far from standing above him, is under his authority as the Son of Man and Messiah. The witnesses agree this both settles the dispute — the Lord of the sabbath may surely declare what is lawful upon it — and reveals, in Mark’s emphatic ‘even,’ the magnitude of the claim: he who made the sabbath for man is himself its master, with the right not to abrogate but to interpret and fulfil it. Robertson stresses that Mark’s ‘even’ shows the magnitude of the claim — the Son of Man master, not slave, of the sabbath; Wesley that the supreme Lawgiver may dispense with his own law; Clarke warns against reading ‘man’ generally, lest the sabbath’s obligation be annihilated. The witnesses agree Christ crowns his defence with a claim of lordship over the sabbath.
